article.title: Navia Benefit Solutions Breach Exposes 2.7 Million People, Including HackerOne Employees, Through BOLA Vulnerability
article.summary: A Broken Object Level Authorization flaw at benefits administrator Navia exposed Social Security numbers and personal data for 2.7 million individuals, with downstream victims including employees of bug bounty giant HackerOne.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Navia Benefit Solutions, a Renton, Washington-based employee benefits administrator, has disclosed a data breach affecting approximately 2.7 million individuals after an unknown attacker exploited a Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) vulnerability to access sensitive records over a 24-day period. The breach has rippled across multiple organizations that rely on Navia for benefits administration, with the most notable downstream victim being HackerOne, the world's largest bug bounty platform.

## What We Know

The unauthorized access occurred between December 22, 2025, and January 15, 2026, according to filings reviewed by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackerone-discloses-employee-data-breach-after-navia-hack/). Navia detected suspicious activity on January 23, 2026, eight days after the intrusion window closed. The company uploaded a substitute breach notice to its website on March 13 and began mailing individual notification letters on March 18.

The compromised data includes Social Security numbers, full names, addresses, phone numbers, dates of birth, email addresses, and plan enrollment and termination dates, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackerone-discloses-employee-data-breach-after-navia-hack/). Navia has stated that claims data and bank account information were not accessed.

HackerOne disclosed on March 24, 2026, that 287 of its employees and their dependents were among those affected, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackerone-discloses-employee-data-breach-after-navia-hack/). The bug bounty platform, which manages over 1,950 programs for clients including the U.S. Department of Defense, General Motors, Goldman Sachs, Anthropic, GitHub, and Uber, confirmed that its own systems were not compromised. The breach occurred entirely within Navia's infrastructure.

Navia has offered affected individuals 12 months of complimentary credit monitoring and identity theft protection services, as reported by [TechRadar](https://www.techradar.com/pro/security/hackerone-says-employees-hit-by-data-breach-and-navia-hack-is-to-blame).

## What We Don't Know

Several questions remain unanswered. Navia has not disclosed the full list of organizations whose employees were affected beyond HackerOne, nor has it clarified whether the 2.7 million figure represents unique individuals or includes duplicate records across multiple employer clients. The identity and motivation of the threat actor remain unknown, and it is unclear whether the stolen data has appeared on dark web marketplaces or been used in subsequent attacks.

The specific technical details of the BOLA vulnerability have not been published, making it difficult to assess whether similar flaws persist in Navia's systems or in comparable benefits administration platforms.

## Analysis

The incident underscores a persistent weakness in enterprise security: the supply chain. Organizations that invest heavily in their own cybersecurity posture remain vulnerable to breaches at third-party vendors that handle sensitive employee data. HackerOne, a company whose entire business model centers on finding and remediating security vulnerabilities, was exposed not through any failure of its own infrastructure but through a benefits administrator's API flaw.

BOLA vulnerabilities, which allow authenticated users to access objects belonging to other users by manipulating identifiers in API requests, have ranked among the most common and dangerous API security issues for years. The OWASP API Security Top 10 lists Broken Object Level Authorization as the number one API threat, yet incidents like this suggest that many organizations processing sensitive data have not implemented adequate object-level access controls.

The 24-day dwell time between initial access and detection, followed by a two-month gap before public notification, also raises questions about breach detection capabilities and notification timelines at benefits administrators that operate outside the stricter regulatory frameworks governing healthcare providers and financial institutions.

Navia serves industries spanning insurance, consumer finance, higher education, and real estate, meaning the downstream impact likely extends well beyond the organizations that have publicly acknowledged being affected.
